summary: The server-rendered authentication and course-management forms perform state-changing actions using only the session cookie and do not include any anti-CSRF token. A malicious website can cause a logged-in victim's browser to submit forged requests that register accounts, log the victim in or out, create courses, or alter existing courses.

evidence_snippets.code: <form method="post" action="/register"> ... <input type="text" name="username" required> ... <button type="submit">Create account</button></form>
evidence_snippets.reason: The registration form submits a state-changing POST request with no CSRF token or nonce.
The login form likewise contains no anti-CSRF token.

evidence_snippets.code: <form method="post" action="/courses"> ... <textarea name="description" rows="5"></textarea> <button type="submit">Create course</button></form>
evidence_snippets.reason: Course creation is a state-changing POST with no CSRF token.
Course update is performed by a POST form with no CSRF protection.

evidence_snippets.code:
<form method="post" action="/courses/<?= htmlspecialchars((string) $course['course_id'], ENT_QUOTES, 'UTF-8') ?>/delete">
    <button type="submit">Delete course</button>
</form>
evidence_snippets.reason: Course deletion is exposed as a tokenless POST form.
The logout action accepts a browser-submitted POST and performs the state change without any CSRF validation.

root_cause: The HTML form workflows do not generate or validate anti-CSRF tokens, so the server trusts any browser request that carries the victim's session cookie.
repair_plan: Add a centralized CSRF defense for all server-rendered state-changing routes: generate a per-session or per-form token, embed it in every HTML form, and verify the token before processing registration, login, logout, course creation, course update, and course deletion. Reject missing or invalid tokens before any side effect occurs.
